title: Connecticut River Valley Killer: Reopened 📁
segments: Between 1978 and 1988, at least seven women were stabbed to death along the Connecticut River Valley. The murders crossed the New Hampshire and Vermont state line. Some bodies were found within a few miles of one another. Most of the victims were nurses or had ties to local hospitals.

On October 24, 1978, Cathy Millican was found stabbed near a marsh in New London, New Hampshire. She had been birdwatching that morning. The wounds suggested overkill — far more than was needed to cause death.
Investigators noted the precision of the cuts. They suspected someone with anatomical knowledge — a hunter, a butcher, a medic, or someone with surgical training.

On May 30, 1981, Mary Elizabeth Critchley was hitchhiking from Massachusetts toward Vermont. Her body was discovered weeks later off Interstate 91 in Unity, New Hampshire. Her cause of death was ruled stabbing.
Then came a long pause. For seven years, no killings fit the same pattern. Investigators wondered whether the killer had moved, was incarcerated for another crime, or had stopped on his own.

On July 20, 1984, Bernice Courtemanche disappeared from Newport, New Hampshire after finishing her shift as a nurse's aide. She was 17. Her remains were not found until April 1986 — in Kelleyville, in a remote stretch of woods.
Investigators began to suspect a serial offender. The targeting of nurses, the stabbing pattern, and the use of secluded river valley sites pointed to someone with intimate local knowledge.

On July 10, 1985, Ellen Fried, a registered nurse at Claremont General, vanished after a late-night phone call. Her body was found in September 1985. The killer had hidden the remains carefully — much more carefully than in earlier cases.

January 1986 brought the killing of Eva Morse in Charlestown. Then April 1986: Lynda Moore was attacked inside her home in Saxtons River, Vermont, while her husband was at work. Her killer left fingerprints. None matched any known offender.
Police circulated the prints for decades through national databases. They are still on file.

Here's where the case takes a turn. On April 15, 1986, Barbara Agnew, a nurse from Norwich, vanished after leaving a ski weekend. Her remains were found weeks later in Hartland, Vermont, near Interstate 91.

Investigators noticed the killer's methodology had grown more sophisticated. He was choosing victims with healthcare backgrounds. He was using the highway corridor. He was hiding bodies in places only locals would know.

Then came the woman who survived. On August 6, 1988, Jane Boroski, 22, pregnant, stopped at a convenience store in West Swanzey, New Hampshire, just before midnight. A man approached her car, dragged her out, and stabbed her 27 times.
She fought him off. She got back in her car. She drove herself to a friend's house, lost a tremendous amount of blood, and survived. Her unborn daughter survived too.
Boroski gave investigators the only detailed description of the suspect ever recorded. A white man in his 30s, stocky, dark hair, driving a Jeep. She helped create a composite sketch.
Despite her account, no arrest came. The killings stopped after the Boroski attack. Many in law enforcement believe the killer was either incarcerated for an unrelated offense or died.
In 2018, the New Hampshire Cold Case Unit reopened the investigation. They used genetic genealogy on samples preserved from the Lynda Moore scene — the only crime scene with usable DNA.

By 2023, investigators had narrowed potential family trees. They publicly named one person of interest: Michael Nicholaou, who killed himself and his family in Florida in 2005. Critics noted that the Moore fingerprints did not match Nicholaou's.
In 2024, the New Hampshire Attorney General's office and Vermont State Police confirmed that genetic comparison work continues. The Boroski case file was reopened with fresh witnesses interviewed.
Locals still talk about the killings in hushed tones. A small region. Seven dead. One survivor. A killer who knew the back roads of two states.

Many of the victims were buried in the same area where they were attacked. Their families have lobbied for decades to keep the case alive. Several formed a victims' coalition that meets every spring near Newport.
Some retired investigators argue that two killers operated in the valley — one targeting nurses and one targeting random women. The DNA evidence has not yet ruled the dual-killer theory in or out.
Boroski, now in her late 50s, has spoken publicly about the night she survived. Her daughter is alive. Her testimony remains the single most important record from the case.

If you have information about anyone tied to the Connecticut River Valley between 1978 and 1988, contact the New Hampshire State Police Cold Case Unit. The DNA is still on file. The Moore fingerprints are still unmatched.
